title: The Somatic Experience of Financial Abundance
body:
The Somatic Experience of Financial Abundance

Here's the thing, you're probably chasing money, but what you're really running from is a feeling. The knot in your stomach when the bill arrives. The tightness in your chest when you check your bank balance. That's not just stress, my friend. That's your body screaming. That's the somatic experience of your money wound. You think money problems are about numbers on a screen, about budgets and investments. And sure, those things matter. But they're symptoms, not the root cause. The money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet. It lives in your nervous system. It's a primal fear, a survival instinct twisted by generations of scarcity and societal conditioning. And until you address that, until you feel it and move through it, you'll always be fighting an uphill battle, no matter how much you earn. The poverty consciousness doesn't care how much you earn. It'll find a way to make you feel poor, to make you feel unsafe, even with millions in the bank. Right?!

Beyond the Spreadsheet: Your Body Holds the Score

You've heard it a thousand times: "Just make more money." Or "Budget better." "Cut expenses." And while some of that advice has its place - I'm not saying financial literacy is useless - it often misses the whole point. It's like telling someone with a broken leg to just walk it off. Your relationship with money isn't just cognitive. It's deeply emotional, and therefore, deeply somatic. Your body is a living archive. Every experience you've ever had, especially those tinged with fear, shame, or anxiety around money, is stored there. It's in the way your shoulders hunch when you talk about debt. It's in the shallow breath you take when you contemplate a big purchase. It's in the clenching of your jaw when you hear someone else brag about their wealth. These aren't just random physical reactions. They're your body's way of communicating unresolved trauma, deep-seated beliefs, and inherited patterns around money. You feel that?

Think about it. When you feel financially secure, truly secure, how does your body respond? There's a lightness, a sense of expansion, perhaps a deeper breath, a more relaxed posture. When you feel financially insecure, it's the opposite: contraction, tension, a feeling of being weighed down. This isn't coincidence. This is biology. Your nervous system is constantly assessing your safety, and money, in our modern world, is inextricably linked to that sense of safety. When your financial situation feels precarious, your nervous system goes into fight, flight, or freeze. Your body prepares for danger. This isn't a metaphor. This is a physiological reality. And you can't out-think a physiological response. You have to feel it, acknowledge it, and then re-pattern it.

This is why simply following advice from someone like Dave Ramsey, while well-intentioned, often falls short for so many. His approach is rooted in shame and deprivation, which only reinforces the nervous system's danger response. It tells your body, "You're bad. You're wrong. You must suffer to be good." That's not healing. That's just layering more trauma on top of existing wounds. It creates a cycle of self-punishment that's unsustainable for most people. True financial abundance isn't about white-knuckling your way to a debt-free life through sheer willpower. It's about creating a state of inner safety and spaciousness that allows wealth to flow to you, and through you, with ease.

Money as Energy: Shifting Your Internal Narrative

Money is just energy with a story attached to it. That's it. Nothing more, nothing less. But oh, the stories we tell ourselves! "I'm not good enough for money." "Money is evil." "Rich people are greedy." "There's never enough." These aren't just thoughts. They're energetic constructs that shape your reality. They become your operating system. And your body, being a magnificent antenna, picks up on these stories and manifests them physically. If your story is "money is scarce," your body will feel scarce. It will hold onto resources, contract, and resist expansion. If your story is "money is a source of joy and freedom," your body will reflect that with openness and ease.

This isn't some airy-fairy, woo-woo concept. This is fundamental psychology. Brad Klontz, a pioneer in financial psychology, talks extensively about "money scripts" - unconscious beliefs about money that we learn in childhood and that dictate our financial behaviors as adults. These scripts aren't just in your head. They're deeply embedded in your psyche and, yes, in your soma. They influence everything from how you save, spend, invest, and even how you talk about money. If your parents constantly worried about money, you probably inherited a "money vigilance" script, causing you to be overly anxious and fearful about your finances, regardless of your actual income. If money was a source of conflict in your home, you might unconsciously sabotage your own financial success to avoid repeating those patterns. Know what I mean?

The work here isn't about positive affirmations alone. It's about excavating those deep-seated stories and feeling the emotional charge they hold in your body. It's about noticing the clench, the tremor, the avoidance. And then, gently, compassionately, inviting a new story, a new energetic signature, into your system. It's about understanding that every penny you earn, every dollar you spend, every financial decision you make, carries an energetic imprint. And when you align that energy with abundance, with generosity, with flow, your body starts to relax, and your financial reality begins to shift.

Geneen Roth, in her incredible book Lost and Found (paid link), talks about how our relationship with food mirrors our relationship with life itself. The same is true for money. Our patterns of restriction, binging, and self-sabotage around food often reflect similar patterns around money. It's all connected. It's all about how we experience worthiness, safety, and pleasure. When you feel worthy of abundance, your body relaxes into it. When you believe you're not, your body holds itself back, creating a self-fulfilling prophecy of lack.

The Practice of Somatic Financial Healing

So, how do you actually do this? How do you heal the somatic money wound? It's not a quick fix. It's a journey of deep self-inquiry and embodied practice. It requires courage, patience, and a willingness to feel uncomfortable. Because to heal, you have to feel. As the title of the book Feelings Buried Alive Never Die (paid link) suggests, those repressed emotions don't just disappear. They manifest as physical symptoms, as financial blocks, as chronic stress. You can't bypass your body on the path to financial freedom.

Here are some practices to begin:

  • Mindful Money Check-ins: Before you open a bill, before you check your bank account, before you make a purchase, pause. Take a few deep breaths. Close your eyes. Notice what's happening in your body. Is there tension? Where? What emotion is present? Don't judge it. Just observe. This simple act of awareness starts to create a gap between the stimulus and your automatic reaction.
  • Pendulation: This is a powerful somatic tool. When you feel the contraction of financial fear, gently bring your attention to a part of your body that feels neutral or even pleasant. It could be the warmth of your hands, the softness of your breath, the feeling of your feet on the ground. Then, gently, bring your awareness back to the uncomfortable sensation. Move back and forth between the discomfort and the resource. This teaches your nervous system that you can tolerate and regulate difficult feelings, expanding your capacity for both discomfort and ease.
  • Embodied Gratitude: Instead of just thinking "I'm grateful," feel it in your body. Find something, anything, you're truly grateful for that day - a hot cup of coffee, a warm bed, clean water. And then locate that feeling of gratitude in your body. Does it feel expansive? Warm? Tingly? Practice this daily. This builds a felt sense of abundance, regardless of your current financial situation.
  • Re-patterning Money Memories: Think back to a time you felt really stressed or shamed about money. Close your eyes. Replay the memory, but this time, imagine yourself as an adult, present and grounded. What would you say to your younger self? How would you hold them? How would you create a sense of safety? This isn't about changing the past, but about re-integrating those fragmented parts of yourself with compassion.
  • Conscious Spending & Receiving: When you spend money, do so with intention. Feel the exchange of energy. When you receive money, whether it's a paycheck or a gift, pause and feel the influx of energy. Don't just dismiss it. Allow yourself to fully receive. This cultivates a healthier energetic flow.

These practices aren't about avoiding the practical aspects of money management. You still need to budget. You still need to save. You still need to invest. But you'll do it from a place of inner resourcefulness and calm, rather than from a place of fear and scarcity. Alan Watts, the great philosopher, often spoke about the illusion of separation. We think we are separate from our money, separate from our circumstances. But it's all one continuous flow. When you align your inner state with abundance, the outer world begins to reflect that alignment.

The Myth of External Abundance First

Many people believe they'll feel abundant once they have a certain amount of money in the bank. "Once I hit six figures, then I'll relax." "Once I pay off my mortgage, then I'll be happy." But this is a trap. It's putting the cart before the horse. True abundance is an internal state, a feeling, a somatic experience. It's not an external achievement. You can have all the money in the world and still feel utterly impoverished inside. We see it all the time with lottery winners who end up bankrupt and miserable. Why? Because they didn't heal the internal poverty consciousness. They didn't address the underlying somatic wound. The money just amplified their existing patterns.

Conversely, you can have very little in terms of material possessions and still feel incredibly rich in spirit, rich in connection, rich in life experience. This isn't to say that external wealth isn't desirable or that you shouldn't strive for financial security. Not at all. It's simply to say that the feeling of abundance doesn't magically appear with more zeros in your bank account. It's something you cultivate from the inside out. It's a vibration you embody. When you embody it, the external circumstances often follow.

This is where books like Sacred Success (paid link) become so valuable. They move beyond the purely transactional view of money and invite you to see it as a spiritual tool, a means for greater service and connection. They challenge you to integrate your spiritual self with your financial self, to bring consciousness to every aspect of your money journey. Because if your spiritual practice isn't touching your money, it's not truly integrated. And if your money isn't serving your highest values, it's not truly abundant.

The journey isn't always easy. It involves confronting your deepest fears, your shame, your guilt around money. It involves looking at how your family system, your culture, and society have shaped your relationship with wealth. It involves recognizing that your nervous system has been wired for survival, and that wiring needs to be consciously updated. It's about slowly, gently, patiently teaching your body that it's safe to have money, safe to receive, safe to expand. Does that land?

The Power of Conscious Consumption

Your relationship with money isn't just about how much you have, it's also about how you use it. Every dollar you spend is a vote, an energetic investment in the world you want to create. This is where conscious consumption comes in. When you buy things mindlessly, out of impulse, or to fill an emotional void, you're often reinforcing patterns of lack and dissatisfaction. You're trying to buy a feeling, but the feeling never lasts because the underlying wound hasn't been addressed.

However, when you spend money consciously, with intention and awareness, it becomes an act of self-care, a way to support your values, and a contribution to the world. Before you buy something, pause. Ask yourself: "Why am I buying this? What feeling am I hoping to get from this? Do I truly need this? Does this align with my values?" This isn't about deprivation. It's about strength. It's about taking back control from unconscious impulses and aligning your spending with your highest self. This practice also helps you to become more attuned to your body's true needs versus fleeting desires. You start to notice when you're trying to numb a feeling with a purchase, or when you're genuinely investing in something that brings you joy or serves a true purpose.

This same principle applies to receiving. How do you receive compliments? How do you receive help? How do you receive gifts? If you're constantly deflecting or minimizing, you're sending a signal to your nervous system that you're not worthy of receiving. This creates an energetic block to financial abundance. Learning to receive gracefully, with an open heart and an open body, is a crucial part of somatic financial healing. It teaches your body that it's safe to let good things in, safe to be supported, safe to be nourished. It's an often-overlooked aspect of wealth creation, yet it's incredibly powerful. Most people are far better at giving than receiving, and that imbalance creates a bottleneck in the flow of abundance.

FAQ

What if I have debt? Can I still feel abundant?

Absolutely. Abundance isn't about the absence of debt; it's about the presence of peace and spaciousness within you, regardless of your external circumstances. You can approach your debt from a place of fear and shame, or you can approach it from a place of empowered action and self-compassion. The somatic work helps you shift that internal state. When you're calm and grounded, you're better equipped to make smart decisions about your debt, rather than reacting from panic. It's about changing your relationship with debt, seeing it as a temporary energetic commitment rather than a permanent burden.

Is this just positive thinking?

No, it's far deeper than just positive thinking. Positive thinking can be helpful, but it often stays in the cognitive realm. Somatic financial healing involves engaging your body, your emotions, and your nervous system. It's about feeling and releasing trapped energy, re-patterning unconscious beliefs at a cellular level, and cultivating a felt sense of safety and abundance. It's an embodied practice, not just a mental one. It's the difference between saying "I am wealthy" and truly feeling wealthy in your bones.

How long does this process take?

Healing is not linear, and it's not a race. This is a lifelong journey of self-awareness and conscious living. You'll have breakthroughs, and you'll have moments where old patterns resurface. The key is consistency and compassion. Every time you bring awareness to your body's response to money, every time you choose a conscious action over an unconscious reaction, you're building new neural pathways and strengthening your capacity for abundance. Small, consistent steps lead to real shifts over time. Don't look for an endpoint; look for the unfolding.

So, where do you start? You start small. You start right now. Don't wait until you've "fixed" your finances. Don't wait until you have "enough." Start by simply noticing. Notice the sensation in your body the next time you think about money. Is it a clench? A flutter? A sinking feeling? Just notice. Don't try to change it. Don't judge it. Just be present with it for five breaths. That's your first step. That's your invitation to your body to begin the conversation. That's how you begin to heal. That's how you reclaim your power. That's how you step into true financial abundance, from the inside out.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Discover how your body holds financial stress. Learn to release tension and cultivate a feeling of abundance, transforming your relationship with money.
author: Kalesh
